schedule_title: The States and the Union territories
broad_content: Lists the States and Union territories of India and their territorial descriptions.
upsc_angle: State reorganisation, Union territories, Article 1, Article 3, Article 4, map-based polity questions.
key_traps: Article 3 changes state boundaries; Article 4 says such laws are not Article 368 amendments.
India is a Union of States, not a federation of states.
Union territories are also part of the territory of India.
current_linkage: Linked with creation/alteration of Union territories, Delhi governance, Jammu and Kashmir reorganisation, and demands for new States.
memory_trigger: First Schedule = Political map of India.
plain_language: Lists the States and Union territories of India and their territorial descriptions.
full_text: Lists the States and Union territories of India and their territorial descriptions.

schedule_title: Provisions as to the President, Governors, Speakers, Judges, CAG and related constitutional offices
broad_content: Deals with salaries, allowances, privileges and conditions of service of major constitutional authorities.
upsc_angle: Constitutional offices, charged expenditure, independence of judiciary and CAG, Speaker/Chairman provisions.
key_traps: Salary provisions are constitutional, but amounts may be changed by law.
Judges’ salaries and CAG expenses are linked with independence.
Do not confuse Second Schedule with Third Schedule oaths.
current_linkage: Relevant in debates on judicial independence, CAG independence and salaries of constitutional authorities.
plain_language: Deals with salaries, allowances, privileges and conditions of service of major constitutional authorities.
full_text: Deals with salaries, allowances, privileges and conditions of service of major constitutional authorities.

broad_content: Contains oath or affirmation forms for Union Ministers, candidates for Parliament, MPs, Supreme Court judges, CAG, State Ministers, State legislators and High Court judges.
upsc_angle: Oath-taking authorities, constitutional posts, sovereignty and integrity phrase, difference between oath of office and secrecy.
key_traps: President’s oath is under Article 60, not Third Schedule.
Governor’s oath is under Article 159, not Third Schedule.
MPs and MLAs take oath before sitting/voting; violation attracts Article 104 or Article 193 penalty.
current_linkage: Relevant whenever appointments to constitutional offices, disqualification, or oath controversies appear in news.
memory_trigger: Third Schedule = Oaths, but not President/Governor.
plain_language: Contains oath or affirmation forms for Union Ministers, candidates for Parliament, MPs, Supreme Court judges, CAG, State Ministers, State legislators and High Court judges.
full_text: Contains oath or affirmation forms for Union Ministers, candidates for Parliament, MPs, Supreme Court judges, CAG, State Ministers, State legislators and High Court judges.

schedule_title: Allocation of seats in the Council of States
broad_content: Allocates Rajya Sabha seats to States and Union territories.
upsc_angle: Rajya Sabha composition, unequal state representation, population-based allocation, federalism.
key_traps: Rajya Sabha does not give equal representation to all States.
Fourth Schedule is amended when State boundaries or status change.
Article 80 governs Rajya Sabha composition; Fourth Schedule gives allocation.
current_linkage: Relevant when new States/UTs are created or Rajya Sabha seat distribution is discussed.
plain_language: Allocates Rajya Sabha seats to States and Union territories.
full_text: Allocates Rajya Sabha seats to States and Union territories.

schedule_title: Administration and control of Scheduled Areas and Scheduled Tribes
broad_content: Deals with Scheduled Areas and Scheduled Tribes in States other than those covered by the Sixth Schedule.
upsc_angle: Scheduled Areas, Governor’s report, Tribes Advisory Council, PESA, tribal autonomy, land alienation.
key_traps: Fifth Schedule applies to Scheduled Areas in several States; Sixth Schedule applies to tribal areas of specific North-Eastern States.
Governor has special responsibility in Fifth Schedule areas.
President declares Scheduled Areas.
current_linkage: Linked with PESA implementation, forest rights, tribal land issues, mining in tribal areas and Governor’s role.
memory_trigger: Fifth Schedule = Scheduled Areas outside Sixth Schedule States.
plain_language: Deals with Scheduled Areas and Scheduled Tribes in States other than those covered by the Sixth Schedule.
full_text: Deals with Scheduled Areas and Scheduled Tribes in States other than those covered by the Sixth Schedule.

schedule_title: Administration of Tribal Areas in Assam, Meghalaya, Tripura and Mizoram
broad_content: Provides for Autonomous District Councils and Regional Councils in tribal areas of Assam, Meghalaya, Tripura and Mizoram.
upsc_angle: Autonomous councils, tribal self-governance, North-East polity, legislative/judicial/financial powers of councils.
key_traps: Sixth Schedule does not apply to all North-Eastern States.
It applies to Assam, Meghalaya, Tripura and Mizoram only.
Autonomous councils have law-making powers on specified local subjects.
current_linkage: Linked with demands for inclusion/exclusion from Sixth Schedule, autonomy movements and North-East governance.
memory_trigger: Sixth Schedule = Autonomous District Councils in four NE States.
plain_language: Provides for Autonomous District Councils and Regional Councils in tribal areas of Assam, Meghalaya, Tripura and Mizoram.
full_text: Provides for Autonomous District Councils and Regional Councils in tribal areas of Assam, Meghalaya, Tripura and Mizoram.

schedule_title: Union List, State List and Concurrent List
broad_content: Distributes legislative subjects between Union, States and both jointly through three lists.
upsc_angle: Federalism, legislative competence, residuary powers, Centre-State conflict, GST, criminal law, agriculture, police, public order.

union_list.description: Subjects on which Parliament has exclusive power, such as defence, foreign affairs, banking, currency, atomic energy, railways and citizenship.
union_list.upsc_trap: Residuary subjects belong to Parliament, not States.

state_list.description: Subjects normally under State legislatures, such as public order, police, public health, agriculture, local government and land.
state_list.upsc_trap: Parliament can legislate on State List subjects in special situations under Articles 249, 250, 252 and 253.

concurrent_list.description: Subjects where both Parliament and States can legislate, such as criminal law, marriage, education, forests, labour welfare and electricity.
concurrent_list.upsc_trap: In case of conflict, Article 254 generally gives Union law primacy, subject to exceptions.

current_linkage: Linked with GST, farm laws debate, education policy, criminal law reforms, public health federalism and environmental regulation.
memory_trigger: Seventh Schedule = Who makes law on what.
plain_language: Distributes legislative subjects between Union, States and both jointly through three lists.
full_text: Distributes legislative subjects between Union, States and both jointly through three lists.

upsc_angle: Official language, linguistic diversity, constitutional recognition, language movements.
key_traps: There are 22 languages in the Eighth Schedule.
English is not in the Eighth Schedule.
Sindhi was added by the 21st Amendment; Konkani, Manipuri and Nepali by the 71st; Bodo, Dogri, Maithili and Santali by the 92nd.
current_linkage: Linked with demands for inclusion of more languages such as Bhojpuri, Rajasthani and others.

schedule_title: Validation of certain Acts and Regulations
broad_content: Contains laws given protection from challenge on the ground of violation of Fundamental Rights, subject to basic structure review.
upsc_angle: Land reforms, judicial review, basic structure, Article 31B, I.R. Coelho judgment.
key_traps: Ninth Schedule is not completely immune from judicial review.
Post-24 April 1973 insertions can be tested against basic structure.
Article 31B is linked to Ninth Schedule.
landmark_cases: Kesavananda Bharati v. State of Kerala
Waman Rao v. Union of India
I.R. Coelho v. State of Tamil Nadu
current_linkage: Relevant whenever Parliament or States seek to protect reservation or land laws from judicial challenge.
memory_trigger: Ninth Schedule = Protected laws, but not above basic structure.
plain_language: Contains laws given protection from challenge on the ground of violation of Fundamental Rights, subject to basic structure review.
full_text: Contains laws given protection from challenge on the ground of violation of Fundamental Rights, subject to basic structure review.

schedule_title: Provisions as to disqualification on ground of defection
broad_content: Contains anti-defection law provisions for MPs and State legislators.
upsc_angle: Anti-defection, Speaker’s role, merger, whip, voluntary giving up membership, judicial review.
key_traps: Disqualification is decided by Speaker/Chairman, but decision is subject to judicial review.
Voluntarily giving up membership is wider than formal resignation.
Merger exception requires two-thirds of members of the legislative party.
landmark_cases: Rajendra Singh Rana v. Swami Prasad Maurya
current_linkage: Highly relevant due to frequent government formation disputes, party splits and Speaker delay controversies.
plain_language: Contains anti-defection law provisions for MPs and State legislators.
full_text: Contains anti-defection law provisions for MPs and State legislators.

broad_content: Lists 29 subjects that may be devolved to Panchayats.
upsc_angle: 73rd Amendment, decentralisation, Gram Sabha, local planning, State Finance Commission.
key_traps: Eleventh Schedule subjects are not automatically transferred; State law devolves powers.
Panchayats are under Part IX.
State Election Commission conducts Panchayat elections.
current_linkage: Linked with rural development schemes, Gram Panchayat finance, localisation of SDGs and digital Panchayat governance.
plain_language: Lists 29 subjects that may be devolved to Panchayats.
full_text: Lists 29 subjects that may be devolved to Panchayats.

broad_content: Lists 18 subjects that may be devolved to Municipalities.
upsc_angle: 74th Amendment, urban local bodies, municipal finance, Metropolitan Planning Committee, District Planning Committee.
key_traps: Twelfth Schedule subjects are not automatically transferred; State law devolves powers.
Municipalities are under Part IXA.
District Planning Committee is Article 243ZD; Metropolitan Planning Committee is Article 243ZE.
current_linkage: Linked with urbanisation, municipal finance, Smart Cities Mission, AMRUT and solid waste management.
plain_language: Lists 18 subjects that may be devolved to Municipalities.
full_text: Lists 18 subjects that may be devolved to Municipalities.
